title: Borescopes, Fiberscopes, and Remote Visual Inspection Systems
narrationScript:
Plenty of hardware cannot be examined eye to surface. Turbine blades inside an engine. Tubes in a heat exchanger. The far side of a vessel nozzle. For those, we send the optics in and bring the image out. This lesson covers the instrument families that do it: rigid borescopes, flexible fiberscopes, video borescopes, and full remote visual inspection systems. One ground rule before any of them touch the work. When a remote or indirect technique substitutes for direct viewing, it must resolve detail at least as well as your eye would up close. You prove that with a resolution target, a card with fine lines or characters, viewed at the working distance before the exam starts.

The rigid borescope is the straight-line tool. Picture a stainless tube. At the far tip sits an objective lens that forms an image. A train of relay lenses carries that image stage by stage up the tube to the eyepiece at your eye. The better instruments use rod lenses, long glass rods that pass more light and give a sharper, brighter picture. Around the lens train, a sleeve of fine glass fibers pipes illumination from a light source down to the tip. Rigid scopes run from about one millimeter to nineteen millimeters in diameter, and they only go where a straight path goes. The big selection question is direction of view. A forward view of zero degrees looks straight ahead as you advance down a bore. A forward-oblique view, about forty-five degrees, looks ahead and outward, which suits corners and threaded holes. A side view at ninety degrees faces the wall right beside the tip, the natural choice for cylinder walls and bore surfaces. A retro view looks back over the scope's shoulder, around one hundred ten to one hundred twenty degrees, to see the near side of a feature you just passed through. And remember, image size changes as the tip moves closer or farther. A plain borescope image carries no built-in scale.

When the path bends, you need a fiberscope. Glass fiber carries light by total internal reflection. The fiber core has a higher refractive index than the cladding around it, so light entering within the acceptance angle reflects off the boundary again and again and follows every bend. Carrying an image takes more than one fiber. It takes a coherent bundle, thousands of fibers arranged so each one keeps the same relative position at both ends. Each fiber delivers one dot of the picture, which is why a fiberscope image has a fine honeycomb texture. The illumination travels through a second, incoherent bundle, where fiber positions are random. Random is fine for light. It is useless for a picture. Abuse a fiberscope and fibers snap. Each broken fiber becomes a permanent black dot in your image. Enough of them, and the scope can no longer pass a resolution check. The tip articulates in two or four directions by control cables, so you can steer through the part.

The video borescope changes the architecture. Instead of carrying the image optically, it converts the image to electronics right at the tip. A miniature CCD or CMOS sensor sits behind the tip lens, with LED lighting beside it. The picture travels up the tube as an electrical signal to a processor and a display. Length stops being an optical problem, the whole crew can watch the screen, and you can freeze, record, annotate, and archive. Modern video borescopes also measure. Comparison measurement scales off a reference of known size placed in the same plane as the indication. Shadow and stereo methods triangulate distance. Phase and structured-light systems project a pattern and build a three-dimensional map of the surface. Every one of those depends on calibration and on tip-to-target distance, so verify the system against its calibration block before you report a measurement.

Above the handheld scale sit remote visual inspection systems. Pan-tilt-zoom cameras lowered into tanks and vessels. Motorized crawlers that drive cameras and lights down miles of pipe while logging distance. Push cameras for small lines. Drones for stacks and roofs. These systems exist to keep people out of confined spaces, out of radiation fields, and off scaffolding, while still delivering a code-quality examination. The requirements do not relax. Prove resolution with a target. Verify lighting at the surface. Calibrate any on-screen measurement, and record where every image was taken.

Last, handling. Never force an insertion tube. Respect the minimum bend radius. Check temperature and chemical compatibility before the tip goes in. Around engines and airframes, control foreign objects. Tether what goes in and count it back out. And clean and verify your scope before and after every job, because the scope you grab tomorrow is only as good as the care it got today.

sections.heading: When the Eye Cannot Get There
sections.body:
A large share of real inspection targets offer no direct line of sight: gas turbine internals, heat exchanger tubing, vessel interiors, gearbox cavities, cast passages. For those we use translated or indirect visual testing — instruments that carry the image from the surface to the examiner. Codes accept this on one condition: the remote technique must demonstrate **resolution at least equivalent to direct visual examination**. In practice you prove that with a resolution target — a card with fine line pairs or characters of known size — placed at the working distance and viewed through the system before the examination begins.

Four instrument families do this work:

- **Rigid borescopes** — straight-line optical tubes with the best image quality per dollar.
- **Fiberscopes** — flexible scopes that carry the image through a coherent fiber bundle.
- **Video borescopes** — flexible scopes with an electronic imager at the tip.
- **Remote visual inspection (RVI) systems** — cameras with their own locomotion and lighting: crawlers, pan-tilt-zoom heads, push cameras, drones.

Selection comes down to the access path (straight or tortuous), the distance, the image quality required, and how the results must be documented.

sections.heading: Rigid Borescopes
sections.body:
A rigid borescope is a precision optical instrument in a stainless tube. The **objective lens** at the distal tip forms an image of the surface. A **relay lens train** carries that image stage by stage up the tube to the **eyepiece**, where your eye (or a camera adapter) views it. Premium instruments use the rod-lens design — long glass rods in place of thin air-spaced lenses — which passes more light and delivers a noticeably brighter, sharper picture. Illumination fibers packed around the lens train pipe light from an external source or built-in LED down to the tip.

Typical diameters run about 1 mm to 19 mm, with working lengths up to roughly 1.5 m — and the scope only goes where a straight path exists. The defining selection choice is **direction of view (DOV)**:

- **Forward (0 degrees)** — look straight ahead while advancing down a bore.
- **Forward-oblique (about 45 degrees)** — ahead and outward; suits corners, threads, and counterbores.
- **Side (90 degrees)** — faces the wall beside the tip; the choice for cylinder walls and bore surfaces.
- **Retro (about 110-120 degrees)** — looks back over the scope's shoulder at the near side of features just passed.

Field of view typically runs 40 to 90 degrees, and image magnification changes continuously with tip-to-target distance — a plain borescope image has no built-in scale. Treat the tube kindly: bending a rigid scope cracks relay lenses, and that damage is rarely economical to repair.

sections.caption: A rigid borescope relays the image from the objective lens to the eyepiece through a rod-lens train, and the tip prism sets the direction of view from forward through retro.

sections.heading: Fiberscopes
sections.body:
When the access path bends, the fiberscope takes over. Its working principle is **total internal reflection**: each glass fiber has a core with a higher refractive index than its cladding, so light entering within the fiber's acceptance angle reflects off the core-cladding boundary over and over and follows the fiber around every bend.

One fiber carries light, not a picture. Imaging requires a **coherent bundle** — thousands of fibers, each about 8 to 10 micrometers across, arranged so every fiber occupies the same relative position at both ends. Each fiber then delivers one picture element, which is why a fiberscope image shows a fine honeycomb texture, and why resolution is set by fiber size and count. Illumination travels through a separate **incoherent bundle**, where fiber positions are random — fine for delivering light, useless for an image, and much cheaper to make.

The distal tip articulates in two or four directions, steered by control cables from knobs or levers on the body, so you can navigate the scope through a part and aim the view. Fiberscopes are durable only when respected: every snapped fiber becomes a permanent black dot in the image, the dots accumulate over the scope's life, and a scope with too many can no longer pass its resolution check. Observe the minimum bend radius, store the scope loosely coiled, never crush or pinch the insertion tube, and handle it by the body — not by yanking the tube.

sections.title: Coherent vs Incoherent Bundles
sections.body: **Coherent bundle:** fibers keep the same relative position at both ends — it carries the **image**, one fiber per picture element. **Incoherent bundle:** fiber positions are random — it carries **light only**. A scrambled bundle can still illuminate, but it can never transmit a picture.

sections.heading: Video Borescopes
sections.body:
The video borescope replaces the optical image path with electronics. A miniature **CCD or CMOS imager** sits directly behind the tip lens — the chip-on-tip design — with LED illumination beside it (or light delivered by fiber). The image is converted to an electrical signal at the tip and travels up the insertion tube by wire to a processor and display.

That architecture changes what is possible. Length is no longer limited by optical losses, so long video probes serve piping and large structures. The whole crew views the same screen. You can freeze the image, capture stills and video, annotate findings, and archive everything for trending — a documentation capability fiber optics never offered.

Modern systems also **measure** on the image:

- **Comparison** — scale from a reference of known size placed in the same plane as the indication.
- **Shadow** — a projected shadow line whose position reveals tip-to-target distance.
- **Stereo** — two offset views triangulate each point.
- **Structured light / phase** — a projected pattern builds a full 3-D map of the surface.

Every method depends on calibrated measurement tips and accurate tip-to-target distance, so verify against the manufacturer's calibration block before reporting any measured value — and re-verify if the tip takes a hit.

sections.caption: A fiberscope carries the picture itself through a coherent fiber bundle, while a video borescope converts the image to an electrical signal at the tip with a CCD/CMOS sensor.

sections.heading: Remote Visual Inspection Systems
sections.body:
When the job outgrows a handheld scope, remote visual inspection (RVI) systems bring their own locomotion, lighting, and recording:

- **Pan-tilt-zoom (PTZ) cameras** lowered into tanks, vessels, and shafts on poles or cables.
- **Pipe crawlers** — motorized carriages with lights, a PTZ head, and a distance encoder, driving miles of pipeline and logging position as they go.
- **Push cameras** for small-bore lines and tubing.
- **Magnetic crawlers** that climb tank shells and structural steel.
- **Drones (UAS)** for stacks, flare tips, roofs, and elevated structure; **submersible ROVs** for flooded structures.

RVI is chosen when sending a person is the wrong answer: it eliminates confined-space entry, keeps dose as low as reasonably achievable in radiation areas, removes scaffolding and rope-access cost, and reaches distances and environments people cannot. The examination requirements do not relax because a robot holds the camera. You still demonstrate **resolution equivalent to direct viewing** with a target at the working distance, verify adequate lighting at the surface, calibrate any on-screen measurement, and tie every image to a location using encoders or reference features so findings can be found again.

sections.title: Scope and RVI Handling Precautions
sections.body:
- **Never force an insertion tube.** If it stops, back off and re-steer — forcing breaks fibers, lenses, and articulation cables.
- **Respect the minimum bend radius** and store flexible scopes loosely coiled.
- **Check the environment first**: temperature limits, fuels and solvents that attack the jacket, and pressure or purge requirements.
- **Electrical hazard**: confirm equipment is de-energized and verify lockout before inserting a conductive scope into machinery or switchgear.
- **FOD control**: around engines and airframes, tether everything that enters a cavity and count tools and tips back out — a lost tip adapter is a foreign object.
- **Verify before and after**: clean the optics and run a resolution check at the start and end of the job; log growing black-dot counts on fiberscopes.

checkQuestions.question: A machined hydraulic cylinder bore with straight-line access needs the sharpest possible image of its sidewall. The best choice is:
options: A rigid rod-lens borescope with a 90-degree side view
A fiberscope with four-way articulation
A push camera
A flat inspection mirror and flashlight
checkQuestions.explanation: Straight access means a rigid scope can be used, and rod-lens optics give the best image quality of any scope type; a 90-degree direction of view faces the bore wall directly.

checkQuestions.question: Why must the image-carrying bundle in a fiberscope be coherent?
options: Coherent glass transmits more light than incoherent glass
Each fiber carries one picture element, so fibers must keep the same relative position at both ends or the picture scrambles
Coherent bundles are more flexible than incoherent bundles
Incoherent fibers cannot carry white light
checkQuestions.explanation: An image is a spatial pattern. Only a bundle whose fibers occupy identical relative positions at both ends preserves that pattern; a random (incoherent) bundle delivers light but scrambles any picture.

checkQuestions.question: Fixed black dots that stay in the same place in a fiberscope image no matter where the tip points indicate:
options: Dirt on the part surface
Insufficient illumination
Broken fibers in the coherent image bundle
A failing CMOS sensor
checkQuestions.explanation: Each broken fiber in the coherent bundle permanently blanks one picture element, so the dots stay fixed in the image regardless of what the scope views. Accumulating dots eventually fail the resolution check.

checkQuestions.question: The fundamental difference between a video borescope and a fiberscope is that the video borescope:
options: Uses a longer coherent bundle
Cannot be articulated
Requires no illumination at the tip
Converts the image to an electrical signal at the tip with a CCD/CMOS sensor instead of relaying it optically
checkQuestions.explanation: The chip-on-tip design digitizes the image at the distal end, so length is no longer limited by optical losses and the image can be displayed, recorded, and measured electronically.

checkQuestions.question: Before a remote visual examination is used in place of direct visual examination, most codes expect you to demonstrate:
options: Resolution at least equivalent to direct viewing, using a resolution target at the working distance
That the camera records in color
A magnification of at least 10x
That the scope diameter is less than 6 mm
checkQuestions.explanation: The governing principle for remote VT is equivalence: the system must resolve detail at least as well as the eye would directly, proven with a fine-line or character resolution target before the exam.

references: CP-105-2024 VT Topical Outline, Level I, 4.3 Borescopes, 4.4 Fiberscopes, 4.5 Video borescopes, 4.6 Remote visual inspection systems
Codes such as ASME BPVC Section V, Article 9 (remote examination resolution equivalence, referenced generically)
Manufacturer operating and care manuals for scope equipment (bend radius, temperature, and calibration verification requirements)

simulator.title: RVI Trainer: Scan the Pipe Bore
simulator.intro: You're inspecting the inside of a process pipe with a video borescope. The bore is dark until your scope reaches it — sweep systematically (don't jump around) and click every indication you find.

targets.label: Linear indication at 10 o'clock
targets.feedback: A linear indication along the surface — record clock position, axial location, and length, then move on without losing your scan pattern.

targets.label: Pitting cluster near the bottom
targets.feedback: Localized pitting — note the cluster boundary and the deepest pit; depth may need a measurement-capable scope tip.

targets.label: Indication at the far weld
targets.feedback: An indication at the girth weld — slow down at every weld; that's where the action is.
